text:
Here's a funny defense of eating dessert first:

Let's be honest, life is short and unpredictable. You could go to bed tonight and wake up in a completely different timeline where dessert doesn't exist anymore. Maybe a meteor strikes the chocolate factory. Maybe society collapses and we're all eating insects by next Tuesday. In that scenario, you'll be lying on your bug-filled mattress thinking, "Gosh, I really wish I'd eaten that tiramisu when I had the chance instead of choking down that lukewarm chicken breast like some kind of obedient medieval peasant."

Dessert first is also just smart resource management. If you eat your vegetables first, you'll be full by the time you get to the good stuff. You'll have like three bites of cake and think, "Well, I guess that's enough." But if you flip the script? You eat the cake, you're satisfied, and then the vegetables are just a nice crunchy palette cleanser. It's basically the same thing restaurants do with their tasting menus, except I'm in charge and also my tasting menu is mostly sugar.

Plus, there's a psychological element here. Eating dessert first makes every other food seem disappointing by comparison, which means you eat less overall. I'm basically doing intermittent fasting, but make it dessert. My doctor doesn't understand this yet, but she will eventually.

And finally, the real argument: conventional meal structure was invented by people who had absolutely nothing going on. They needed to create arbitrary rules to make eating interesting. But we live in a golden age of chaos. We can stream anything, we can work from anywhere, we can wear pajamas to important meetings. Eating dessert first isn't rebellious anymore—it's just accepting that we've already thrown the playbook out the window.

The vegetables will still be there when you're done. They're not going anywhere. They're literally vegetables.
